item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a magician IRL and I spend a lot of time being miffed by how magic, as depicted in fiction, is frequently off. So, it came as a great pleasure to read this. You seem to understand much how a magic actually reads and what sort of effects magicians like. There's a great emotional timbre to the whole thing. I give you a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: for me, the saddest part is that the book was successfully and completely (if you believe the article) suppressed. his daughter will never know that her father devoted the last months, maybe years of her life, blowing through his savings just to try to win her back, or at least make sure that she knows he loves her. what a bummer. :-(

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
I was already in love at the I/O NEWT, and then the final line was SO clinical that I cackled because I'm a terrible person who cackles at unbelievably sad stories.

Here's your adaptation!
https://youtu.be/j2tpGA52wKI

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't want to go too in-detail regarding the effects out of concern of getting lost in the weeds, but referencing another document is a good solution — I'll give it some thought and see if there's a way to implement it cleanly — thanks!

(Also, it ties into my head-canon that some of these effects are memorized by Foundation agents for in-field use. There's gotta be a benefit to being able to fold paper in such a way that you 'overwrite' one side with what's on the other side)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one. It's sort of similar to SCP-433 in that the "object" isn't actually something physical, but rather a set of instructions to create an anomalous effect. That said, it goes in a very different direction that I really liked.

I'd like to see some kind of text about the nature the anomalies themselves, theories as to why they work, that sort of thing, but it's not a huge deal. Maybe even just a single sentence to the effect of "See Document 3079-R for research notes pertaining to the nature of anomalies" or something to indicate that it's something that's being looked into.

The emotional aspect I thought was a bit heavyhanded, but overall it worked well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was thinking it was his daughter. Bringing back a lost pet, lamenting missed birthdays, thinking about how much she must have grown since last seen. Sounds a lot more like a daughter than an ex.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally thought the ending was more funny than sad, like the whole thing was a stunt to get back with his ex.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't like this one. While it is of a good quality, the main anomaly isn't at all that interesting, and the backstory behind it feels so contrived it's pretty much what secured my downvote.

There are good things about this though. Despite the anomaly not being interesting, it has potential. And I'm glad there's still some sort of in-universe explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this. Any skip that can provide an in-universe explanation for its existence gets a big boost toward a +1 from me, and this does so.

My only minor quibble is that it sounds like each one of these tricks could almost be considered a separate anomaly, as there's nothing that suggests they rely on the book itself to function. Ie; if somebody read the book, learned a trick, and demonstrated and taught it to somebody else who has never seen the book, does the anomaly not now exist entirely independently? Not saying they need their own SCP designation, but maybe number them as 3079-1, -2, etc.

Overall a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautiful. I initially thought it would be another generic magic books, which it is, but the story woven was woven into it in such a way that in the end I felt this mixture of sadness and happiness. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I/O-NEWT

fuck youuuuu

As for the anomaly itself, I'm… hm. On one hand, I genuinely made a little sad noise at the end of this, and on the other, I've become kind of inured to this particular variety of feels-affecting skips. Gonna novote for now, but I'm leaning towards an upvote for concise storytelling and the good kind of Series I feel.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Got me feeling like I'll never give up
Got me feeling like I'll never give up on you

SOURCES:

  • Image taken from a pdf (here) in the public domain.

Like the article? Click here for more of The Great Hippo's Skippos!
